card.front: What is the central Freakonomics claim about incentives, and the three types?
card.back: People respond to incentives, often in unintended ways. Three types: economic (money), social (reputation/approval), and moral (conscience). Designing incentives badly invites gaming.
The value of the next-best alternative given up when a choice is made. The true cost of anything is what you forgo to get it — not just the price paid.

card.front: What does 'thinking at the margin' mean?
card.back: Decisions compare marginal benefit vs marginal cost of one more unit, not totals/averages. Act when MB > MC; stop where MB = MC.

card.front: Why doesn't correlation imply causation, and what can explain a spurious correlation?
card.back: Two variables can move together due to reverse causation or a confounder (a third variable driving both). Establishing causation needs experiments or natural experiments / instruments.

card.front: Define moral hazard with an example.
card.back: When a party takes more risk because it doesn't bear the full cost. Example: insured drivers driving less carefully; hidden actions after a contract.

card.front: Define adverse selection and how it differs from moral hazard.
card.back: Adverse selection: hidden information before a deal (the riskiest buy insurance). Moral hazard: hidden action after the deal. Selection is about who participates; hazard is about behavior change.

card.front: What is information asymmetry, and how do experts exploit it?
card.back: One party knows more than the other. Experts (agents, realtors) may exploit an information edge to serve their own incentives — e.g. recommending unnecessary services.

card.front: Give the Freakonomics-style logic of an unintended consequence.
card.back: A well-meant rule changes incentives, producing a perverse outcome — e.g. a daycare fine for late pickup increases lateness by reframing it as a cheap price rather than a moral breach.
